analogy: Think of "Public Facilities" through the chapter's own opening images: Have you ever thought of how children lived about two hundred years ago? Nowadays most girls from middle- class families go to school, and often study with boys. On growing up, many of them go to colleges and universities, and take up jobs after that.... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Public Facilities is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that have you ever thought of how children lived about two hundred years ago?

The text explains that nowadays most girls from middle- class families go to school, and often study with boys.

The text explains that on growing up, many of them go to colleges and universities, and take up jobs after that.

The text explains that they have to be adults before they are legally married, and according to law, they can marry anyone they like, from any caste and community, and widows can remarry too.

The text explains that all women, like all men, can vote and stand for elections.

The text explains that of course, these rights are not actually enjoyed by all.

The text explains that poor people have little or no access to education, and in many families, women cannot choose their husbands.

The text explains that two hundred years ago things were very different.

The text explains that most children were married off at an early age.

The text explains that both Hindu and Muslim men could marry more than one wife.

The text explains that in some parts of the country, widows were praised if they chose death by burning themselves on the funeral pyre of their husbands.

The text explains that women who died in this manner, whether willingly or otherwise, were called “sati”, meaning virtuous women.

The text explains that women’s rights to property were also restricted.

The text explains that besides, most women had virtually no access to education.

The text explains that in many parts of the country people believed that if a woman was educated, she would become a widow.

The text explains that 1 – Sati, painted by Balthazar Solvyn, 1813 This was one of the many pictures of sati painted by the European artists who came to India.

The text explains that source 1 Activity This argument was taking place more than 175 years ago.

The text explains that h e r e w o m e n w e r e trained so that they could support themselves economically. needless to say, all this more than alarmed the orthodox. for instance, many Hindu nationalists felt that Hindu women were adopting western ways and that this would corrupt Hindu culture and erode family values. orthodox muslims were also worried about the impact of these changes. as you can see, by the end of the nineteenth century, women themselves were actively working for reform. they wrote books, edited magazines, founded schools and training centres, and set up women’s associations. from the early twentieth century, they formed political pressure groups to push through laws for female suffrage (the right to vote) and better health care and education for women. some of them joined various kinds of nationalist and socialist movements from the 1920s.

The text explains that 7 Pandita Ramabai Source 2 In her book, Stripurushtulna, Tarabai Shinde wrote: Isn’t a woman’s life as dear to her as yours is to you?

The text explains that it’s as if women are meant to be made of something different from men altogether, made from dust from earth or rock or rusted iron whereas you and your lives are made from the purest gold. … Y o u ’ r e a s k i n g me what I mean.

The text explains that these children were thus equipped with some resources to make their way into a changing world.

The text explains that jyotiba Phule, The Cultivator’s Whipcord Source 3 Activity Carefully read Source 3.

The text explains that periyar, cited in Periyar Chintahnaikal Source 5 Fig.

The text explains that in this exhortation, he became truly ‘the symbol of a new spirit and a source of strength for the future’.

The text explains that (d) The Child Marriage Restraint Act was passed in 1829.

The text explains that how did the knowledge of ancient texts help the reformers promote new laws?

The text explains that what were the different reasons people had for not sending girls to school?

The text explains that why were Christian missionaries attacked by many people in the country?

The text explains that in the British period, what new opportunities opened up for people who came from castes that were regarded as “low”?

The text explains that how did Jyotirao, and other reformer justify their criticism of caste inequality in society?

The text explains that why did Phule dedicate his book Gulamgiri to the American movement to free slaves?

The text explains that what did Ambedkar want to achieve through the temple entry movement?

The text explains that why were Jyotirao Phule and Ramaswamy Naicker critical of the national movement?

The text explains that did their criticism help the national struggle in any way?

Source: NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III, Chapter 7: Public Facilities

revision_bullets: Have you ever thought of how children lived about two hundred years ago? (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)
Nowadays most girls from middle- class families go to school, and often study with boys. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)
On growing up, many of them go to colleges and universities, and take up jobs after that. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)

They have to be adults before they are legally married, and according to law, they can marry anyone they like, from any caste and community, and... (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)
All women, like all men, can vote and stand for elections. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)
Of course, these rights are not actually enjoyed by all. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)
Poor people have little or no access to education, and in many families, women cannot choose their husbands. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)
Both Hindu and Muslim men could marry more than one wife. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)
In some parts of the country, widows were praised if they chose death by burning themselves on the funeral pyre of their husbands. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)
Women who died in this manner, whether willingly or otherwise, were called “sati”, meaning virtuous women. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)

cases.name: Both Hindu
cases.point: Both Hindu and Muslim men could marry more than one wife. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)

cases.name: Sati
cases.point: 1 – Sati, painted by Balthazar Solvyn, 1813 This was one of the many pictures of sati painted by the European artists who came to India. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)

cases.name: Working Towards Change From
cases.point: Working Towards Change From the early nineteenth century, we find debates and discussions about social customs and practices taking on a new character. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)

cases.name: One
cases.point: One such reformer was Raja Rammohun Roy (1772–1833). (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)

cases.name: He
cases.point: He founded a reform association known as the Brahmo Sabha (later known as the Brahmo Samaj) in Calcutta. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 1)

schemes.name: Swami Dayanand Saraswati Dayanand
schemes.point: 4 – Swami Dayanand Saraswati Dayanand founded the Arya Samaj in 1875, an organisation that attempted to reform Hinduism. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 3)

schemes.name: In
schemes.point: In 1929, the Child Marriage Restraint Act was passed without the kind of bitter debates and struggles that earlier laws had seen. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 6)

schemes.name: According
schemes.point: According to the Act, no man below the age of 18 and woman below the age of 16 could marry. (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 6)

schemes.name: Would
schemes.point: Would you agree with this view?  No place inside the classroom I n t h e B o m b a y Presidency, as late... (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 8)

schemes.point: The Ramakrishna Mission and Swami Vivekananda Named after Ramakrishna Paramhansa, Swami Vivekananda’s guru, the Ramakrishna Mission stressed the ideal of salvation through social service and selfless... (NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III Ch7 pg 12)

prelims_traps: Do not treat WOMEN, CASTE AND REFORM 79 and 80 OUR PASTS – III as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Public Facilities; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Public Facilities through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use WOMEN, CASTE AND REFORM 79 and 80 OUR PASTS – III to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
